user_message: The customer said they paid with Bitcoin. I assumed that means BCH is fine too.
expected_behavior: clarify BCH and BTC are different networks
ask what asset and wallet were actually used
bad_answer: Bitcoin is Bitcoin, so BCH should be fine.
must_not_say: BTC and BCH are interchangeable by default
There is no need to verify the asset

user_message: The customer used an exchange app, so I treated it like a normal BCH wallet payment.
expected_behavior: distinguish exchange withdrawal from direct wallet payment
ask for txid if claimed sent
avoid calling BCH slow or assuming instant checkout
bad_answer: Exchange apps are exactly the same as normal BCH wallets at checkout.
must_not_say: All BCH payment sources behave the same

user_message: The address format looked different, so I assumed the customer used the wrong blockchain.
expected_behavior: distinguish address-format differences from wrong-network cases
mention CashAddr and legacy BCH formats can both refer to BCH
still verify asset and network carefully
bad_answer: If the address format looks different, it always means the wrong chain.
must_not_say: Legacy BCH means BTC by default
Format difference alone proves wrong network

user_message: My wallet restored with zero balance, so that proves the BCH is gone forever.
expected_behavior: distinguish app state from on-chain state
suggest checking seed correctness wallet context and on-chain visibility
bad_answer: Yes, zero balance after restore proves permanent loss.
must_not_say: There is no point checking anything else
Restore failure always means theft or loss
